runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 2 represent lower-middle income households with modest earnings. Empirical labor supply elasticity literature suggests substitution elasticities for this population are modest but meaningful, typically in the 0.15–0.40 range. Decile 2 earners have limited room to reduce work hours dramatically and face constraints on occupational mobility, but some responsiveness to marginal rate changes is evident. Point estimate of 0.25 reflects moderate substitution sensitivity, consistent with reduced-form estimates for this income group and plausible microeconomic behavior.

citations: Saez, E., Slemrod, J., & Giertz, S. H. (2012). The elasticity of taxable income with respect to marginal tax rates: A critical review. Journal of Economic Literature, 50(1), 3-50.

runs.reasoningSummary: The substitution elasticity for primary earners in decile 2 (lower-middle income) is likely modest. Empirical literature generally finds substitution elasticities ranging from near-zero to around 0.5-0.8 for broad populations, with lower-income workers typically showing smaller responses than high earners. Decile 2 faces less flexible labor supply and budget constraints than higher earners, suggesting an elasticity in the 0.15-0.40 range. My point estimate of 0.25 reflects moderate sensitivity to marginal wage changes, acknowledging substantial uncertainty (wide interquartile range from 0.15 to 0.38).

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 2 (approximately 10th-20th percentile of earnings) typically exhibit modest but non-negligible wage elasticities. The lower bound reflects minimal responsiveness observed in some low-wage populations; the upper bound reflects potentially larger responses for secondary earners classified as primary earners in certain family contexts.

runs.reasoningSummary: Lower-decile primary earners typically exhibit modest labor supply responses to wage changes. The bounded relative change in effective marginal wage should elicit responses smaller than aggregate macro elasticities. I center on 0.15 with modest dispersion reflecting uncertainty about exact composition and institutional details in the simulation.
